children.name: Pandinus imperator
children.description: Largest scorpion by mass, reaching 20 cm and 30 g; native to tropical West African rainforests and savannas. Listed on CITES Appendix II due to heavy collection for the pet trade; despite its imposing size, venom is mild.

children.name: Pandinus cavimanus
children.description: A large East African forest scorpion notable for reddish pincers contrasting with its dark body; inhabits humid woodland and forest floor. Now sometimes placed in genus Pandinoides; widely kept in captivity.

children.name: Pandinus dictator
children.description: A massive black West African forest scorpion rivalling the emperor scorpion in size; sometimes cited as the largest scorpion species by length. Listed on CITES Appendix II due to collection pressure.

children.name: Pandinus gambiensis
children.description: A large West African savanna scorpion of Senegal and the Gambia, sheltering in dense grass and rock crevices during the dry season. Like its relatives, it relies on powerful pincers and has mild venom.

children.name: Pandinus viatoris
children.description: A large East African scorpion related to the emperor, inhabiting savanna and woodland from Kenya to Tanzania. Like its kin it is heavily collected for the pet trade and is CITES-listed.

children.name: Pandinus exitialis
children.description: A forest scorpion of West and Central Africa distinguished by a more potent venom than most Pandinus; inhabits humid lowland forest and gallery woodland. Despite its name, confirmed human fatalities are unverified.

children.name: Pandinus mesaensis
children.description: A savanna-dwelling Pandinus of West Africa, found in rocky plateau habitats and sparse woodland; large-bodied with the characteristic broad pincers of the genus.

children.name: Pandinus pallidus
children.description: A pale-colored, medium-sized Pandinus from West Africa, lighter in hue than most congeners; inhabits drier woodland edges and dry-season savanna grasslands.

children.name: Pandinus roeseli
children.description: A West African forest scorpion named after the naturalist Rösel, occupying humid montane and lowland forest; morphologically close to P. imperator but with distinct chelal dentition.

children.name: Pandinus africanus
children.description: A widespread Central and East African forest scorpion reaching up to 16 cm; inhabits leaf litter and rotting logs in tropical rainforest.

children.name: Pandinus bellicus
children.description: A large, robust Pandinus of West Africa known for aggressive defensive posture; occupies humid forest and gallery woodland near water sources.

children.name: Pandinus magrettii
children.description: An East African Pandinus from the Horn of Africa region, inhabiting savanna and dry woodland; relatively small for the genus with slender chelae.

children.name: Pandinus meidensis
children.description: A Central African forest scorpion of the Congo basin region; large-bodied, dark brown to black, sheltering in humid lowland rainforest floor debris.

children.name: Pandinus militaris
children.description: A robust West African giant scorpion inhabiting humid tropical forest, reaching up to 18 cm; like other Pandinus, it has relatively weak venom but delivers powerful pincer pressure.

children.name: Pandinus phillipsi
children.description: A medium-large East African Pandinus described from Somalia and Kenya, inhabiting dry and semi-arid woodland savanna.

children.name: Pandinus sabaeus
children.description: A Pandinus from Yemen and the Horn of Africa, one of the few Pandinus species occurring in the Arabian Peninsula; inhabits rocky semi-arid terrain and dry woodland.

children.name: Pandinus smithi
children.description: A large East African Pandinus from Ethiopia and Somalia, sheltering under rocks and in loose soil in savanna and semi-arid woodland.

children.name: Pandinus viridimanus
children.description: A West African giant scorpion with greenish-yellow chelae contrasting with a dark body; inhabits humid coastal forest and rainforest margins.

children.name: Pandinus gracilis
children.description: A slender-bodied Pandinus of West Africa, smaller and more lightly built than P. imperator; inhabits forest margins and woody savanna.

children.name: Pandinus colei
children.description: A forest scorpion of Central Africa described by Lowe in 2010; medium-large, dark-bodied, occupying moist forest and woodland zones.

children.name: Pandinus comoriensis
children.description: Endemic to the Comoro Islands in the Indian Ocean, this forest scorpion represents an isolated island population; inhabits humid lowland and mid-altitude forest.

children.name: Pandinus kasumbaensis
children.description: A Pandinus from the Congo Basin forests around Kasumba, large-bodied and dark in color; occupies humid equatorial rainforest.

children.name: Pandinus swazicus
children.description: A southern African Pandinus found in Eswatini and adjacent South Africa, inhabiting subtropical moist forest and rocky woodland hillsides.

children.name: Pandinus jinxiuensis
children.description: A Pandinus described from Jinxiu, Guangxi, China; one of the few Pandinus species recorded from Asia, inhabiting humid subtropical forest.

children.name: Pandinus platychelus
children.description: A large Pandinus of the Guinean forests of West Africa, distinguished by its notably flattened, broad pedipalp chelae which it uses for excavating burrows in the soft earth of the forest floor. Its massive, spade-like claws are a remarkable adaptation for a life spent tunnelling through the thick leaf litter and topsoil of the tropical African lowland rainforest, where the scorpion excavates a shallow retreat under logs, roots, and debris into which it drags its prey — mostly large insects, millipedes, and any small vertebrate unfortunate enough to stray into range of its sting.

children.name: Pandinus magnus
children.description: One of the largest Pandinus species, reaching up to 22 cm in length. Found in the tropical forests of Cameroon, Gabon, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. Its massive size and relatively docile temperament have made it a sought-after species in the exotic pet trade — the same trade that led to CITES Appendix II listing for its better-known relative, the emperor scorpion, and which now drives increasing scrutiny of the international trade in this and other giant African forest scorpions whose slow reproductive rates (females give birth to 12–30 offspring after a gestation of 9–12 months) make them particularly vulnerable to population collapse under sustained collection pressure.

children.name: Pandinus mahagiensis
children.description: A Pandinus described from the Mahagi region on the shores of Lake Albert in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo. Its distribution is poorly known due to the inaccessibility of its type locality in the conflict-affected Ituri region, but it is presumed to inhabit the remaining blocks of lowland rainforest along the Albertine Rift.

children.name: Pandinus pyrrhus
children.description: A reddish-brown Pandinus from the Guinea region of West Africa. Its colouration is unusual among Pandinus, which are typically black or very dark brown.

children.name: Pandinus ultrabscurus
children.description: An exceptionally dark, almost velvet-black Pandinus from the forests of Cameroon and Gabon. Its deep black colour is matched only by the faintest blue sheen in direct sunlight.

children.name: Pandinus imperator subtylo
children.description: A recently distinguished form related to the emperor scorpion. Its type locality is in the rainforests of southern Cameroon.

children.name: Pandinus mecus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus micheli
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus nistratus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus percivali
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus pinnatus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus platycheles
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus pococki
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus provocator
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus pygmaeus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus richteri
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus serratus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus subovalis
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus triaena
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus ulderigonii
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Pandinus chevalieri
children.description: A large West African emperor scorpion from Senegal and Guinea-Bissau, inhabiting savanna woodland and gallery forest. Named after Auguste Chevalier, the French botanist and explorer who collected specimens in West Africa.

children.name: Pandinus cristimanus
children.description: A West African Pandinus from Nigeria and Cameroon, distinguished by a prominent crest or ridge on the inner palm of the chelae. It inhabits humid lowland forest and gallery woodland along major river systems.

children.name: Heterometrus spinifer
children.description: A large scorpion of Southeast Asian forests reaching up to 15 cm; relies more on pincers than venom and glows brilliantly under UV light. Widely kept as a pet and exported in large numbers from Thailand and Indonesia.

children.name: Heterometrus laoticus
children.description: Among the largest scorpions in Asia, found in Laos, Thailand, and Vietnam in humid lowland and montane forest. Hides in tree bark crevices and deep leaf litter; often mistaken for H. spinifer in trade.

children.name: Heterometrus swammerdami
children.description: Often cited as the world's longest scorpion species, reaching approximately 23 cm; native to India and Sri Lanka in humid lowland forest. Powerful pincers and mild sting make it a popular display animal.

children.name: Heterometrus longimanus
children.description: A glossy black rainforest scorpion of Southeast Asia, widespread from Myanmar through Malaysia; common in the pet trade. Defensive posturing is frequent, but venom toxicity is low.

children.name: Heterometrus cyaneus
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion with a distinctive bluish-black iridescent sheen, inhabiting humid leaf litter and self-dug burrows. Occurs across the Philippines and surrounding island chains.

children.name: Heterometrus petersii
children.description: A large forest scorpion of southern China and Indochina recognized by golden-yellow legs contrasting its dark body; popular as a hardy terrarium species. Inhabits humid subtropical and tropical forests.

children.name: Heterometrus silenus
children.description: A dark, heavy-bodied forest scorpion from the Philippines, occurring in humid lowland forest leaf litter and under rotting logs. One of the more frequently encountered Heterometrus in the Philippine archipelago.

children.name: Heterometrus indus
children.description: A glossy black forest scorpion of the Indian subcontinent and Sri Lanka, commonly found in plantations and forest floor habitats. One of the most medically significant Heterometrus due to its proximity to human dwellings.

children.name: Heterometrus laevigatus
children.description: A large, smooth-bodied forest scorpion from Sulawesi and nearby Indonesian islands, dwelling in humid lowland rainforest. Its smooth cuticle distinguishes it from many rougher-surfaced congeners.

children.name: Heterometrus mysorensis
children.description: A heavy-bodied forest scorpion from the Mysore Plateau and adjacent Western Ghats of southern India, sheltering under stones and logs. One of several Heterometrus species described from the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Heterometrus fulvipes
children.description: A forest scorpion of peninsular India distinguished by tawny or reddish-brown legs on an otherwise dark body; inhabits humid lowland and mid-elevation forests. Common around human habitation in Kerala and Tamil Nadu.

children.name: Heterometrus madraspatensis
children.description: An Indian forest scorpion first described from the Chennai (Madras) region; occurs in the Eastern Ghats and Deccan coastal scrub forest, sheltering under rocks and bark.

children.name: Heterometrus granulomanus
children.description: A Heterometrus identified by strongly granular palmar surfaces; occurs in the Western Ghats of India in moist deciduous and rainforest habitats. The granulate chelae assist grip on prey in humid leaf litter.

children.name: Heterometrus nepalensis
children.description: A forest scorpion of the Himalayan foothills and Nepal terai; one of the northernmost Heterometrus species, inhabiting warm subtropical forest at lower elevations.

children.name: Heterometrus xanthopus
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion with bright yellow legs and pedipalps on a dark body; occurs in Thailand, Myanmar, and adjacent countries in moist lowland forest.

children.name: Heterometrus bengalensis
children.description: A medium-large dark forest scorpion of West Bengal and Bangladesh, inhabiting moist deciduous forest and riverside woodland. One of the commoner Heterometrus in the Bengal plain.

children.name: Heterometrus kanarensis
children.description: A forest scorpion of the Kanara (North Karnataka) coast and Western Ghats, inhabiting humid evergreen and semi-evergreen forest. Medium-sized for the genus with moderately granulate chelae.

children.name: Heterometrus thorellii
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion described by Pocock and named for the arachnologist Tord Tamerlan Theodor Thorell; occurs in Myanmar and northern Indochina in humid forest.

children.name: Heterometrus scaber
children.description: A Heterometrus recognizable by its distinctly rough, scabrous cuticle texture compared to more polished congeners; occurs in South Asian forest floor habitats.

children.name: Heterometrus verdezi
children.description: A Heterometrus described from mainland Southeast Asia, forming part of the diverse radiation of large Asian forest scorpions; inhabits humid tropical lowland forest.

children.name: Heterometrus phipsoni
children.description: An Indian forest scorpion described from the Mumbai region; occurs in the northern and central Western Ghats in moist deciduous forest.

children.name: Heterometrus laoensis
children.description: A Heterometrus endemic to Laos, described from specimens collected in humid montane and lowland tropical forest; distinguished from H. laoticus by chelal and metasomal morphology.

children.name: Heterometrus lowei
children.description: A recently described Asian forest scorpion named for scorpiologist Graeme Lowe; inhabits moist forest of the Indochina subregion.

children.name: Heterometrus cimrmani
children.description: A Heterometrus described from Southeast Asia; large-bodied and dark, occupying humid lowland rainforest in the Mekong subregion.

children.name: Heterometrus boehmi
children.description: An Asian Heterometrus from the broader Southeast Asian forest zone; medium to large in size, sheltering under fallen logs and leaf litter in humid forest.

children.name: Heterometrus gigas
children.description: One of the largest Heterometrus species from Sulawesi, Indonesia; a deep rainforest inhabitant with massive chelae relative to body size. Poorly known outside Indonesian collections.

children.name: Heterometrus liophysa
children.description: A Heterometrus of South or Southeast Asia characterized by a notably smooth dorsal surface; inhabits moist tropical forest and is occasionally exported in the pet trade.

children.name: Heterometrus fissipalmus
children.description: A Heterometrus of South Asia characterized by a distinctive notch or division in the palm surface of the chelae; inhabits humid forest floor habitats in the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Heterometrus ramachandra
children.description: An Indian forest scorpion described from the eastern Western Ghats; medium-large, dark-bodied, occurring in moist deciduous forest and adjacent rocky terrain.

children.name: Heterometrus peterfordii
children.description: A recently described species from Vietnam. Named after the arachnologist Peter Ford. Little is yet known about its ecology.

children.name: Heterometrus gajardoi
children.description: A Heterometrus of Sumatra, described in the early 20th century and recently rediscovered. Its conservation status is unknown but it is likely restricted to the increasingly fragmented lowland rainforests of Sumatra.

children.name: Heterometrus magnus
children.description: Arguably the largest Heterometrus, reaching 17 cm. It inhabits the rainforests of Borneo and Sumatra, where it lives in burrows excavated in the soft clay soils of the forest floor beneath the massive dipterocarp trees of the Sundaland lowlands. Its diet consists of large insects, centipedes, and occasionally small lizards and frogs, which it subdues with its massive pincers and a quick flick of the metasoma, typically requiring venom only for the most tenacious prey.

children.name: Heterometrus maurus
children.description: A dark, almost uniformly black Heterometrus from Thailand and Cambodia. Its name means 'dark' or 'gloomy' — a reference to both its colour and its hidden, nocturnal habits.

children.name: Heterometrus flavipes
children.description: Named for the yellowish-brown colouration of the legs and telson, which contrast with the black body. Found in Myanmar, Thailand, and the Andaman Islands.

children.name: Heterometrus xystus
children.description: A moderately large Heterometrus described from Burma (Myanmar) with distinctive coarse granulation on the carapace. It inhabits the dry deciduous forests of central Myanmar, a markedly more arid environment than the humid rainforests occupied by most of its congeners.

children.name: Heterometrus petilus
children.description: A relatively slender Heterometrus from Thailand, with a more elongate metasoma than other members of the genus. Its slender build may reflect a more active, wide-ranging foraging strategy compared to the ambush-hunting of the larger, bulkier species.

children.name: Heterometrus aporus
children.description: A Heterometrus of the Thai-Malay peninsula with notably smooth, poreless pedipalps. Its skin texture distinguishes it from the more granulated Heterometrus species of mainland Southeast Asia.

children.name: Heterometrus bispinosus
children.description: A species from Sumatra and Borneo with two distinctive spines on the telson vesicle.

children.name: Heterometrus celebensis
children.description: A Heterometrus endemic to Sulawesi, Indonesia. Its isolated position on this geologically unique island has produced morphological differences from its Sundaland relatives.

children.name: Heterometrus ockenius
children.description: A medium-sized Heterometrus of the Philippines. Its pincers are proportionally smaller than those of continental Asian species.

children.name: Heterometrus philippinus
children.description: A dark black Heterometrus native to the Philippines. Its distribution is limited to the islands of Luzon and Mindoro.

children.name: Heterometrus sulatrensis
children.description: A large Heterometrus from the island of Sulawesi. Its taxonomic status is debated.

children.name: Heterometrus trichodactylus
children.description: A small Heterometrus named for the dense covering of fine hairs on its pedipalp fingers. Found in the forests of Thailand.

children.name: Heterometrus vespertinus
children.description: A species of Heterometrus from the Sunda Islands, named for its exclusively nocturnal activity.

children.name: Heterometrus xanthodes
children.description: A Heterometrus of Java and Bali with yellowish-brown colouration on the telson and metasomal segments.

children.name: Heterometrus barberi
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus borneensis
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus brevimanus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus ceylonicus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus laevipes
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus liangi
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus minotaurus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus robustus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus tristis
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Heterometrus australimanus
children.description: A Heterometrus of peninsular Malaysia and southern Thailand inhabiting humid lowland forest. Distinguished by the form of the ventral surface of the chelal palm; it shelters under logs and in leaf litter, reaching about 12 cm.

children.name: Heterometrus gravimanus
children.description: A large, heavy-chelate Heterometrus of South India, distinguished by notably massive, broad pedipalps relative to body length. It inhabits moist deciduous and semi-evergreen forests of the Western Ghats.

children.name: Heterometrus nanchenensis
children.description: A Heterometrus from Yunnan Province, southwestern China, one of the northernmost Asian forest scorpions. It inhabits humid subtropical forest in the foothills and lower montane zones, sheltering under stones and logs.

children.name: Heterometrus spinisoma
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion identified by spinose granulation on the dorsal metasomal segments; occurs in humid tropical forest of Thailand and Cambodia. Like most Heterometrus, it relies heavily on its pincers when subduing prey.

children.name: Heterometrus sabariensis
children.description: A forest scorpion of the Sabari hills and adjacent Western Ghats of Kerala, India. Medium-large with dark coloration, it inhabits moist evergreen forest floor, emerging after monsoon rains in large numbers.

children.name: Scorpio maurus
children.description: A stout, sand-colored burrowing scorpion of North Africa and the Middle East with very large pincers relative to body size; excavates spiral burrows up to 50 cm deep in arid soil. Has been studied as a model for arthropod burrowing mechanics.

children.name: Scorpio palmatus
children.description: A golden, broad-clawed desert scorpion of the Levant and Sinai that excavates deep vertical burrows in sandy or loamy desert soils. Its venom is mild relative to sympatric buthid species.

children.name: Scorpio fuscus
children.description: A burrowing scorpion of the eastern Mediterranean hills, recently separated from Scorpio maurus on morphological and molecular grounds. Digs burrows into firm hillside soils in Israel, Lebanon, and Syria.

children.name: Scorpio kruglovi
children.description: A North African and Middle Eastern burrowing scorpion distinguished from S. maurus by chelal and trichobothrial characters; found in Algeria, Libya, and adjacent regions digging burrows in gravelly desert soil.

children.name: Scorpio occidentalis
children.description: The westernmost Scorpio species, occurring in Morocco and western Algeria; distinguished from other Scorpio by morphological characters and restricted to semi-arid Atlantic coastal habitats.

children.name: Scorpio granulatus
children.description: A North African Scorpio with a noticeably granulate carapace and chelae; inhabits sandy and rocky desert zones of northeastern Africa and digs characteristic spiral burrows.

children.name: Scorpio fuliginosus
children.description: A darkly pigmented North African Scorpio from Tunisia and Libya; darker in overall coloration than most congeners, inhabiting semi-arid steppe with gravelly soils.

children.name: Scorpio propinquus
children.description: A burrowing scorpion of the Egyptian Eastern Desert and Sinai Peninsula, closely related to S. maurus but with distinct chelal morphology. It excavates deep spiral burrows in sandy and gravelly desert soils.

children.name: Scorpio aequalis
children.description: A Scorpio of the Jordan Valley and adjacent lowlands, one of several species segregated from the former Scorpio maurus complex through morphological and molecular analysis. Inhabits loamy soils in semi-arid scrub.

children.name: Scorpio arabicus
children.description: A burrowing scorpion of the Arabian Peninsula, occupying sandy and loamy desert soils in Saudi Arabia and Yemen. One of the few Scorpio species occurring outside Africa, it retains the characteristic broad pincers and mild venom of the genus.

children.name: Scorpio rosei
children.description: A North African Scorpio of Algeria and Tunisia, named after the South African biologist Werner Rose. Recognized following the revision of the S. maurus complex, it inhabits semi-arid steppe soils in the Maghreb region.

children.name: Scorpio socotranus
children.description: A burrowing scorpion endemic to the island of Socotra in the Indian Ocean, part of the remarkable endemic fauna of this isolated archipelago. Like other Scorpio, it excavates spiral burrows in sandy soil and has relatively mild venom.

children.name: Opistophthalmus capensis
children.description: A robust southern African burrowing scorpion with forward-set eyes adapted to monitoring the burrow entrance for predators and prey. Defensive and quick to stridulate when disturbed.

children.name: Opistophthalmus glabrifrons
children.description: A large southern African scorpion with striking yellow legs on a dark body, digging burrows in hard savanna and Karoo soils. Common in the Karoo biome of South Africa.

children.name: Opistophthalmus boehmi
children.description: An East African burrowing scorpion with a strikingly tri-colored body pattern, inhabiting dry savanna and digging burrows beneath rocks. Named for explorer Richard Böhm.

children.name: Opistophthalmus wahlbergii
children.description: A pale, sturdy burrowing scorpion of the Kalahari, excavating deep spiral burrows in sandy soils as thermal refuges against the desert heat. Named for Swedish naturalist Johan August Wahlberg.

children.name: Opistophthalmus carinatus
children.description: A robust southern African burrowing scorpion with strongly keeled pincers, common in arid and semi-arid savanna soils. Widespread in the Northern Cape and western South Africa.

children.name: Opistophthalmus walbergii
children.description: A common burrowing scorpion of southern African savanna, named after the Swedish naturalist Johan August Wahlberg. Its burrows are a familiar sight in the red earth of Kruger National Park, and the scorpion is frequently seen at night with a UV torch, its cuticle fluorescing a brilliant greenish-blue in the beam.

children.name: Opistophthalmus latimanus
children.description: A heavily built scorpion of the Namib Desert and adjacent arid regions of South Africa and Botswana. Its exceptionally broad, flattened pedipalps are used for shovelling sand and for blocking the entrance of its burrow, forming a near-perfect seal against the desiccating desert air. The broad, flattened pincers, when pressed flat against the scorpion's body as it sits at the mouth of its burrow, create a seal so effective that measurements of water loss through the burrow entrance with the scorpion in place versus empty reveal a reduction of over 90% — a remarkable adaptation to the demands of life in the most arid landscapes of southern Africa.

children.name: Opistophthalmus aurantiacus
children.description: A strikingly coloured Opistophthalmus with a predominantly orange-yellow body and darker markings. Found in the Kalahari Desert of Botswana and Namibia. Its bright colouration may serve as a warning to predators in the open, sparsely vegetated landscape of the Kalahari.

children.name: Opistophthalmus cavimanus
children.description: A southern African species distinguished by a distinctive hollow or depression on the inner surface of its pedipalp chelae. The function of this cavity is debated — it may serve as a resonance chamber for stridulation (most Opistophthalmus species produce a distinctive chirping sound by rubbing a specialised file against the base of the pedipalps when threatened, a behaviour that is unique among scorpions and whose acoustic properties suggest a warning function analogous to the rattle of a rattlesnake).

children.name: Opistophthalmus austroafricanus
children.description: A medium-sized Opistophthalmus from the arid scrublands of the Northern Cape of South Africa and southern Namibia. Its body is relatively slender compared to other members of the genus, allowing it to occupy narrower, shallower burrows in the rocky soils of the Bushmanland region where the stony surface layer prevents the construction of the deep, elaborate tunnels typical of the genus in softer soils.

children.name: Opistophthalmus bainesi
children.description: A large Opistophthalmus from Namibia and Angola. Named after the English painter and explorer Thomas Baines, who documented the natural history of southern Africa in the 19th century.

children.name: Opistophthalmus biserricus
children.description: A southern African Opistophthalmus with a distinctive double row of teeth on the chelicerae, used for digging in compacted soils.

children.name: Opistophthalmus brevicauda
children.description: A burrowing scorpion of the Kalahari Desert with a notably short, robust metasoma. Its compact build is adapted to digging in the hard, calcareous soils of the Kalahari.

children.name: Opistophthalmus coccineus
children.description: One of the most strikingly coloured Opistophthalmus, with a bright reddish-orange body. Found in the Richtersveld region of South Africa.

children.name: Opistophthalmus concolor
children.description: A uniformly dark brown Opistophthalmus of the South African Karoo. Its lack of colour markings distinguishes it from most other species.

children.name: Opistophthalmus dentatus
children.description: A robust Opistophthalmus from Namibia with prominent dentition on the chelicerae.

children.name: Opistophthalmus erinaceus
children.description: A small Opistophthalmus of the Namib Desert covered in unusually dense, bristle-like setae.

children.name: Opistophthalmus gigas
children.description: The largest Opistophthalmus, reaching 17 cm. Found in the arid scrublands of Namibia.

children.name: Opistophthalmus karrooensis
children.description: A species restricted to the Great Karoo of South Africa, adapted to the extreme temperature fluctuations of this semi-desert region.

children.name: Opistophthalmus laticostis
children.description: Named for the prominent, broad carinae (ridges) on its carapace, which reinforce the exoskeleton for digging in rocky soils.

children.name: Opistophthalmus macrorus
children.description: A species with an unusually long, slender metasoma, found in the Kalahari Desert.

children.name: Opistophthalmus austerus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Opistophthalmus boreus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Opistophthalmus concinnus

children.name: Opistophthalmus peringueyi

children.name: Opistophthalmus pretoriae
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Opistophthalmus scabriceps
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Opistophthalmus schultzei
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Opistophthalmus setosus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Opistophthalmus villosus
children.description: A southern African Opistophthalmus distinguished by a notably dense covering of setae (hairs) on the legs and chelae. It inhabits sandy soils in the Little Karoo and adjacent arid zones of the Western Cape.

children.name: Opistophthalmus mugayanus
children.description: An East African Opistophthalmus from the Ugandan savanna region, one of the more northerly members of this otherwise predominantly southern African genus. It excavates burrows in the red laterite soils of the East African plateau.

children.name: Opistophthalmus schlechteri
children.description: A southern African burrowing scorpion from the Namaqualand and Richtersveld regions, inhabiting rocky and sandy soils in the succulent karoo. Named after the botanist Rudolf Schlechter.

children.name: Opistophthalmus tridentatus
children.description: An Opistophthalmus identified by three distinct teeth on the fixed finger of the chelae; found in southern Africa in arid and semi-arid rocky terrain. The distinctive dentition may assist grip on large, hard-bodied prey such as darkling beetles.

children.name: Opistophthalmus natalensis
children.description: A burrowing scorpion of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa, constructing elaborate spiral burrows in the deep loam soils of coastal and midland grasslands. Medium-sized for the genus with robust chelae.

children.name: Opisthacanthus asper
children.description: A flattened, rock-dwelling scorpion of African woodlands that shelters in crevices and under bark rather than digging burrows; the rough cuticle texture gives the species its name. Widespread across sub-Saharan forest zones.

children.name: Opisthacanthus elatus
children.description: A slender, crevice-dwelling scorpion found in Colombia and adjacent South America; the genus Opisthacanthus has a remarkable disjunct distribution spanning Africa and the Americas. Hides under bark and stones in humid tropical forest.

children.name: Opisthacanthus cayaporum
children.description: A South American Opisthacanthus described from Brazil; inhabits humid Atlantic forest and Cerrado woodland, concealing itself beneath bark and in narrow crevices. Part of the remarkable Africa-Americas disjunct distribution of the genus.

children.name: Opisthacanthus lepturus
children.description: A South American creeping scorpion with a notably slender metasoma compared to congeners; found in tropical forest habitats in the Guiana region. Flattened body form facilitates life in tight bark crevices.

children.name: Opisthacanthus madagascariensis
children.description: The sole Opisthacanthus known from Madagascar, inhabiting moist lowland and eastern rainforest; represents a westward island dispersal event from mainland Africa.

children.name: Opisthacanthus mccormicki
children.description: A West African Opisthacanthus described from forest zones of Nigeria and Cameroon; flattened body, hides under loose bark on large trees and fallen logs in humid forest.

children.name: Opisthacanthus rugiceps
children.description: An African creeping scorpion with a notably rugose (wrinkled) carapace surface; known from Central Africa's forest zone, sheltering under bark and in rock fissures.

children.name: Opisthacanthus validus
children.description: A stout-bodied West African Opisthacanthus from Nigeria and Cameroon; larger and more heavily built than many creeping scorpions, inhabiting forest floor debris and rocky crevices in humid lowland forest.

children.name: Opisthacanthus capensis
children.description: A southern African Opisthacanthus from the Cape and KwaZulu-Natal regions; flattened and bark-dwelling, preferring rocky fynbos and coastal forest habitats over the burrowing lifestyle of sympatric scorpions.

children.name: Opisthacanthus africanus
children.description: A widespread creeping scorpion of Central and East Africa, one of the more commonly encountered Opisthacanthus; lives beneath loose bark and in rock crevices in mixed woodland.

children.name: Opisthacanthus flavus
children.description: A yellowish-brown bark scorpion of Brazil and Paraguay, inhabiting humid Atlantic forest and Cerrado woodland. It hides beneath loose bark on large trees and fallen logs, exploiting the same microhabitats as its African relatives.

children.name: Opisthacanthus weyenberghii
children.description: An Argentine and Uruguayan bark scorpion named after the Dutch naturalist Hendrick Weyenbergh. It inhabits the subtropical gallery forests and espinal woodlands of the Río de la Plata basin.

children.name: Opisthacanthus lucasi
children.description: A South American bark scorpion described by Gervais, named after the French naturalist Hippolyte Lucas. It occurs in moist tropical and subtropical forests of Brazil, sheltering under bark and in rotting logs.

children.name: Opisthacanthus havilandi
children.description: A West African bark scorpion described by Pocock, named after the entomologist G.D. Haviland. It inhabits humid lowland forest in Nigeria and Cameroon, living under loose bark on standing and fallen trees.

children.name: Opisthacanthus barberi
children.description: A Central African bark scorpion of the Congo Basin, named after the entomologist H.G. Barber. It lives beneath loose bark on large trees in humid equatorial rainforest, foraging at night on the bark surface for invertebrates.

children.name: Opisthacanthus testaceus
children.description: A reddish-brown (testaceous) bark scorpion of Venezuela and Colombia, inhabiting humid tropical forest and living beneath bark on large trees. It represents the northernmost Opisthacanthus in South America.

children.name: Opisthacanthus acutecarinatus
children.description: An East African bark scorpion distinguished by sharply keeled (acutely carinate) metasomal segments; found in woodland and forest zones of Kenya and Tanzania. Like its relatives, it lives under bark rather than digging burrows.

children.name: Cheloctonus jonesii
children.description: A southern African burrowing scorpion with a long, slender metasoma, inhabiting grasslands and digging shallow burrows in the Highveld and adjacent regions. The only species in its genus.

children.name: Hadogenes troglodytes
children.description: An extremely flattened southern African scorpion that lives in narrow rock cracks and is among the longest scorpions by body length; remarkably long-lived with extremely mild venom. The dorsoventral flattening is an adaptation to exploiting tight rock crevices.

children.name: Hadogenes paucidens
children.description: A flattened crevice-dwelling scorpion of southern African rocky outcrops, sheltering by day in tight cracks and foraging at night on cliff faces. Smaller than H. troglodytes.

children.name: Hadogenes bicolor
children.description: A slim, dorsoventrally compressed scorpion adapted to life within rock fissures of southern Africa, with striking contrasting body and limb coloration. Favors granite and sandstone outcrops.

children.name: Hadogenes gracilis
children.description: A slender, gracile flat rock scorpion of the Eastern Cape and southern KwaZulu-Natal, smaller and more lightly built than H. troglodytes. It exploits narrower crevices in quartzite and sandstone outcrops, foraging on cliff faces at night for invertebrate prey.

children.name: Hadogenes gunningi
children.description: A flat rock scorpion of southern Zimbabwe and northern Mozambique, typically found beneath exfoliating granite slabs on rocky hillsides. Named after H.S. Gunning, a pioneer of South African zoology.

children.name: Hadogenes tityrus
children.description: An East African flat rock scorpion found in Tanzania and Malawi, one of the more northerly-distributed Hadogenes. It inhabits rocky outcrops in miombo woodland, sheltering in deep crevices during the day and emerging at night to hunt on vertical rock surfaces.

children.name: Hadogenes zumpti
children.description: A Namibian and Botswanan flat rock scorpion named after the entomologist Fritz Zumpt. It lives in granite and basalt crevices in semi-arid savanna, tolerating greater aridity than many congeners and emerging to hunt only on warm, humid nights.

children.name: Hadogenes lawrencei
children.description: A flat rock scorpion of the KwaZulu-Natal midlands, described and named after the arachnologist R.F. Lawrence. It is restricted to quartzite and dolerite outcrops within the KwaZulu-Natal Drakensberg foothills.

children.name: Hadogenes newlandsi
children.description: A flat rock scorpion of Zimbabwe and adjacent Zambia, sheltering in granite exfoliations and rock fissures in miombo woodland. Named after the arachnologist J. Newlands.

children.name: Hadogenes weygoldti
children.description: A flat rock scorpion of the Northern Cape and Namaqualand, inhabiting rocky quartzite outcrops in arid fynbos and succulent karoo. Named after the German arachnologist Peter Weygoldt.

children.name: Hadogenes fasciatus
children.description: A flat rock scorpion of the Cape Fold Mountains and southwestern South Africa, distinguished by faint banding on the metasoma. It occupies quartzite and sandstone crevices in the fynbos biome.

children.name: Pandinoides cavimanus
children.description: An East African forest scorpion with a dark body and reddish pincers, formerly placed in Pandinus; common in the terrarium hobby and exported in numbers from East African range countries.

children.name: Liocheles australasiae
children.description: The most widely distributed scorpion in the Old World tropics, occurring across Southeast Asia, Australia, Pacific islands, and introduced to parts of Africa and the Americas. It lives under bark on large trees and in damp log piles; females are parthenogenetic in some populations, producing offspring without mating, a remarkable adaptation that has facilitated its spread across oceanic islands.

children.name: Liocheles waigiensis
children.description: A bark scorpion of New Guinea and the western Pacific islands, closely related to L. australasiae and similarly found under loose bark on forest trees. It has colonised a number of Pacific island groups and contributes to the scant scorpion fauna of Melanesia.

children.name: Liocheles nigripes
children.description: A bark scorpion of peninsular Malaysia and southern Thailand distinguished by darker, nearly black legs contrasting with its pale body. It inhabits humid lowland forest, resting under loose bark on standing and fallen trees during the day.

children.name: Liocheles karschii
children.description: A bark scorpion of Borneo and the Philippines, named after the German arachnologist Ferdinand Karsch. It occurs in lowland and hill dipterocarp forest, sheltering under bark on large trees in humid forest.

children.name: Liocheles longimanus
children.description: A bark scorpion of Myanmar and northern Thailand characterised by relatively long pedipalps compared to congeners. It inhabits deciduous and semi-evergreen forest, where it shelters in loose bark on large trees.

children.name: Hormiurus granulatus
children.description: A South American bark scorpion of Brazil and Uruguay distinguished by granulate cuticle texture, living under loose bark in humid Atlantic forest and subtropical woodlands. Like its African relatives Opisthacanthus, it has a flattened body adapted to exploiting tight bark crevices.

children.name: Hormiurus guianensis
children.description: A bark scorpion of the Guiana Shield region — Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, and northern Brazil — inhabiting humid tropical forest. It shelters under bark on large trees near watercourses and is most commonly found in gallery forest along major rivers.

children.name: Hormiurus borelli
children.description: An Argentine and Bolivian bark scorpion named after the Italian naturalist Alfredo Borelli, who collected extensively in South America in the late 19th century. It inhabits subtropical and tropical dry forest at moderate elevations.

children.name: Hormiurus ochraceus
children.description: An ochre-coloured bark scorpion of the Amazon Basin and adjacent Cerrado of Brazil, living beneath bark on large trees in humid tropical forest. Its pale colouration provides camouflage against the pale inner surface of exfoliating bark.
